lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Aristotle, my dear friend. Let us speak of truth itself. What is the simplest truth you can think of?

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: That a thing is itself, Plato. If it is a horse, it is a horse. If it is a man, it is a man. In the language you have devised: $I \to I$.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Indeed! And how would one **prove** such a thing? We cannot simply declare it true — we must demonstrate it.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: We begin by assuming $I$. From that [assumption|assume], $I$ follows immediately — we just said it was true. Then we [discharge|discharge] the assumption, forming the [implication|implication] $I \to I$.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Excellent. This is the essence of a **[judgement|judgement]**: from a [context|context] of assumptions $\Gamma$, we derive a [conclusion|conclusion] $p$. We write this as $\Gamma \vdash p$.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So the turnstile $\vdash$ divides what we assume from what we prove. Every line in a proof is a judgement.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Precisely. Now, truth is richer than mere identity. We have **conjunction** $A \land B$ — both things hold together. We have **disjunction** $A \lor B$ — at least one holds. We have **negation** $\lnot A$ — the thing is false. And we have the grand **implication** $A \to B$ — if $A$ holds, then $B$ must follow.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: And for each connective, there are rules: how to **introduce** it into a proof, and how to **eliminate** it — to use what it asserts.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Yes! The [introduction rule|introduction rule] tells us how to **build** a formula. The [elimination rule|elimination rule] tells us how to **use** a formula we already have. This is the heart of [natural deduction|natural deduction].

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So a proof is a tree of these rule applications, each step justified by the steps before it?

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Exactly. And here, you will write your proofs as **s-expressions** — commands like `(assume I)` or `(->-intro I 1)`. The machine will check each step. Now, shall we begin?
